metadata.summary: Organize spatial content in three physical zones (near/mid/far) with ergonomic constraints for head-mounted displays. Interactive controls must stay in near/mid field; far field is ambient-only.

content.title: Spatial UX Fundamentals
content.summary: Spatial UX organizes content in three physical zones: near field (0–1 m, primary interactions and controls), mid field (1–3 m, content consumption and work surfaces), far field (3 m+, ambient context and navigation aids). Interactive controls must never be placed in the far field. Use 1.5–2.0 m as default content distance. Provide a recenter affordance. Test seated AND standing — comfortable reach shifts ~15°.

why.p: 2D UI patterns do not translate to 3D spatial interfaces: a button at screen-bottom works on a phone but falls in the chin-fatigue zone on a headset. Field zones enforce ergonomic constraints derived from human-factors research on head-mounted displays. Skipping them produces layouts that users cannot comfortably reach or sustain, causing abandonment after minutes of use.

list.item: Drafting first-pass UX requirements for Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest, or HoloLens apps.
Re-mapping a 2D mobile or desktop flow into near/mid/far field zones.
Reviewing a spatial layout for reach, occlusion, sight-line, and orientation issues.
Establishing shared spatial vocabulary across PM, engineering, and 3D teams.
Phone AR snap-on features (banner overlays) — world-scale and reach constraints don't apply meaningfully.
Desktop 3D viewers (CAD, Blender) — different ergonomic constraints than head-mounted spatial.
360-degree video lean-back content — viewer is passive; no reach or occlusion design is needed.

rule.statement: Organize all spatial content in three physical zones: near field (0.3–1.0 m) for primary interactions and controls, mid field (1.0–3.0 m) for content consumption and work surfaces, far field (3.0 m+) for ambient context and navigation aids only.
rule.rationale: Zones derive from human-factors research on comfortable reach, vergence-accommodation, and sustained viewing distance for head-mounted displays. Placing interactive controls in the far field forces users to walk or stretch, causing abandonment.

rule.statement: Never place interactive controls in the far field. Ambient and contextual content only.
rule.rationale: Users cannot reliably reach or gaze-target elements beyond 3 m on current HMD hardware. Eye-tracking precision drops, hand-ray jitter increases, and small controls become inaccessible.

rule.statement: Use 1.5–2.0 m as the default content distance for any surface requiring sustained viewing (reading, video, dashboard). Test both seated and standing — comfortable reach shifts approximately 15 degrees.
rule.rationale: Both Quest 3 (approx. 110 degree FoV) and Vision Pro (approx. 100 degree FoV) have optical sweet spots near the display center. 1.5–2.0 m places content in comfortable vergence range for most users and reduces eye fatigue during extended sessions.

section.name: Anchoring Types
section.p: Three anchoring modes, each appropriate for different content types:

list.item: Head-locked: content moves with the user's head. Reserve for critical HUD elements (battery warning, notifications). Overuse causes simulator sickness.
Head-locked with decay: content follows the head with a lag, settling into world space after movement stops. Best for primary controls — feels responsive without nausea.
World-locked: content is fixed in physical space. Best for persistent work surfaces (documents, dashboards). Requires a recenter affordance when users drift from the content.
Hand-attached: content follows the hand. Best for tools and context menus. Switch to world-locked when the user stops moving to allow two-handed interaction.

rule.statement: Anchor primary controls as head-locked-with-decay or hand-attached. Avoid pure world-locked for primary controls — they force users to walk back to their content after physical movement.
rule.rationale: Users drift, rooms are reconfigured, and real-world obstacles appear. Primary controls that require physical walking to access are effectively inaccessible in practical use.

rule.statement: Avoid placing critical UI in the lower 30 degrees of the vertical FoV (chin region). This zone causes neck fatigue after repeated downward gaze.
rule.rationale: Looking down more than 30 degrees from horizontal requires neck flexion that fatigues within minutes. Bottom-edge controls on a phone transfer directly to the chin region on an HMD — never port 2D layouts unchanged.

rule.statement: Provide a "recenter" affordance (button or voice command) accessible at all times. Snapping to the home pose recovers content placement when users have drifted.
rule.rationale: Without recenter, users who physically turn or move have no way to re-orient to their content without removing and replacing the headset. This is the most common usability complaint in field testing.

rule.statement: Text placed in the far field (3 m+) must use at minimum 60 pt font for legibility. Text at 2 m should use at minimum 36 pt.
rule.rationale: Angular resolution on current HMDs ranges from 25–35 PPD. Small text that appears readable on a monitor becomes illegible at distance on headset hardware.

antipattern.description: Porting 2D screen layouts directly to spatial without remapping to field zones.
antipattern.reason: 2D patterns assume a fixed viewport. Spatial adds depth, reach, and gaze constraints that invalidate bottom-nav, top-bar, and modal-overlay patterns. Every element must be assigned a field zone explicitly.

antipattern.description: Placing primary CTAs or form controls in the far field (3 m+) to "give the layout breathing room".
antipattern.reason: Far field is for ambient context only. Interactive controls at 3 m+ are unreachable by hand-ray for many users (arm length, reach angle) and untargetable by gaze at typical HMD resolutions.

antipattern.description: Generating 3D assets from LLM text-to-3D for production use.
antipattern.reason: Text-to-3D quality, polygon count, UV mapping, material assignments, and licensing are all unsuitable for production spatial apps. Agents that generate geometry must not bypass the art-direction and budget-validation pipeline.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated USD or glTF with incorrect axis convention (Y-up vs. Z-up) shipped without validation.
antipattern.reason: Axis convention mismatches produce upside-down or sideways models in engine. Validate every generated asset through gltf-validator or usdview before import.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Drive Claude to translate a 2D wireframe into a spatial spec: assign each panel or control to near/mid/far field with rationale, specify world-scale in centimeters, annotate anchoring type, and flag occlusion and reach risks. A second agent runs a heuristic checklist against the spec. Use Reality Composer Pro USDA text format or a JSON spatial spec (not screenshots) as the source-of-truth artifact.

Key gotchas: Claude defaults to thinking in pixels and dp. Force unit normalization to meters in every prompt or output produces nonsense scales. LLMs recommend skeuomorphic 3D buttons — reference platform Human Interface Guidelines (Vision Pro vibrancy, Quest hover states) explicitly in the prompt. Any layout element beyond 2 m or closer than 0.4 m needs a designer with HMD-on review — agents cannot judge eye fatigue or vestibular discomfort.

Anchor primary controls hand-attached or head-locked-with-decay; pure world-locked controls force users to walk. Reserve far field for ambient/contextual content; never put primary CTAs there. Use 1.5-2.0m as default content distance — comfortable for sustained viewing on Quest/Vision Pro. Provide a "recenter" affordance (button or voice) — users drift, and snapping to home pose recovers fast. Test seated AND standing — comfortable reach changes by ~15° and breaks bottom-edge controls. Avoid placing critical UI in the lower 30° of FoV (chin region) — fatiguing to look down repeatedly.

Claude defaults to thinking in pixels/dp; force unit normalization to meters in every prompt or it produces nonsense scales. Generated USD/glTF from LLMs often has incorrect Y-up vs. Z-up — always validate before opening in engine. LLMs over-recommend "skeuomorphic" 3D buttons; remind to follow platform Human Interface Guidelines (Vision Pro vibrancy, hover states). Human-in-loop checkpoint: any layout >2m away or <0.4m needs a designer with HMD-on review — agents cannot judge eye fatigue. Don't let agents auto-generate 3D assets from text-to-3D for production; quality, scale, and licensing all break.

section.name: Limitation Scope
section.p: Field guidance (0–1 m / 1–3 m / 3 m+) assumes seated or standing immersive use. It does not cover: pass-through desk work (different ergonomics), walking AR (dynamic reach envelope), or 360-degree video (passive viewing, no interaction design). Apply these fundamentals only where the user is stationary and actively interacting with spatial content.

Anthropometric averages in field-zone guidelines mask variance: children, wheelchair users, and users with limited arm reach all break "comfortable arm reach" assumptions. Test with at least two anthropometric edge cases per usability cycle.

Fundamentals are device-agnostic; they don't compensate for FoV (Quest 3 ≈ 110°, Vision Pro ≈ 100°) or eye-tracking presence. Static field zones don't account for moving users, vehicles, or multi-room layouts.

template.purpose: Validate JSON spatial spec: flag elements outside correct distance bands or interactive controls in far field
